original_query: Detail the Indian arm wrestling ecosystem: Pro Panja League franchise auction season structure, Indian Armwrestling Federation IAFF, Tamil Nadu Arm Wrestling Association district structure, qualification path district to state to national to international, weight categories used in India, typical entry fees, prize money, operational pain points (manual brackets on paper, weigh-in scale disputes, slow result reporting, lack of video review, payment delays).
contents:
The Pro Panja League (PPL) is run as a franchise‑based competition that began with a public auction of city‑based teams – Punit Balan Studios bought the Mumbai franchise and Gautam Reddy’s group bought Hyderabad in May 2023 ([MediaBrief](https://mediabrief.com/pro-panja-league-punit-balan-acquires-mumbai-franchise/)). The league’s inaugural season ran from 28 July 2023 (broadcast on Sony Ten 1 and DD Sports) and followed a double round‑robin where each of the six teams played every other team twice; the top four advanced to single‑elimination semi‑finals and a final ([Khel Today](https://kheltoday.com/category/pro-panja-league/), [Grokipedia](https://grokipedia.com/page/Pro_Panja_League)). Six men’s weight classes ( < 60 kg, 60‑70 kg, 70‑80 kg, 80‑90 kg, 90‑100 kg, > 100 kg) and three women’s classes ( < 55 kg, 55‑65 kg, > 65 kg) plus an open physically‑challenged division make up the ten categories used in the league and in national competition ([Grokipedia](https://grokipedia.com/page/Pro_Panja_League), [Wikipedia PPL](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pro_Panja_League)). The champion team receives ₹2 million (≈ US $24 000) and the runner‑up ₹500 000 (≈ US $5 900), with equal prize money distributed across all categories ([Wikipedia PPL](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pro_Panja_League)). Entry‑fee amounts for individual athletes are not detailed in the available sources.

The Indian Arm‑Wrestling Federation (IAF), founded in 1977 and headquartered in Chhattisgarh, is the national governing body; it affiliates 28 state associations, runs district‑ and state‑level championships, and selects national teams for international events such as the World Arm‑Wrestling Championships ([Sportsmatik](https://sportsmatik.com/know-how/get-governing-bodies-details/indian-arm-wrestling-federation), [Wikipedia IAF](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_Arm_Wrestling_Federation), [IAF website](http://indianarmwrestling.com/)). The typical qualification pathway is district → state → national → international, with the IAF’s national championships feeding the Indian contingent sent to world championships ([Wikipedia IAF](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_Arm_Wrestling_Federation)).

In Tamil Nadu, the Tamil Nadu Arm Wrestling Association (TN AWA) is the state affiliate of the IAF; its leadership (President Hashim Reza Zabeth, General Secretary Kedar Chandra Sarangi) is listed on the association’s website and Facebook page ([TN AWA site](http://tnarmwrestlingassociation.com/), [Facebook](https://www.facebook.com/TamilnaduArmWrestlingAssociation/)). District bodies such as the Chennai District Arm Wrestling Association organise local championships that serve as qualifiers for the Tamil Nadu State Arm Wrestling Championship (e.g., the 2025 event scheduled for 24‑25 January at Rajalakshmi Engineering College) ([Scribd PDF](https://www.scribd.com/document/819139475/Motivational-story)). Winners progress to the state championship, then to the national level under the IAF’s structure, and ultimately may be selected for international representation.

The sources do not provide specific information on typical entry fees, detailed operational pain points (e.g., manual paper brackets, weigh‑in scale disputes, slow result reporting, lack of video review, or payment delays), or systematic solutions to those issues.

# Pro Panja League

Ara Eve Leo Sal 1x

The **Pro Panja League (PPL)** is India's first professional franchise-based [arm wrestling](https://grokipedia.com/page/Arm_wrestling) league, founded in 2020 by [actors](https://grokipedia.com/page/List_of_Nigerian_actors) Parvin Dabas and Preeti Jhangiani under their production company Swen Entertainment to elevate the traditional sport of *panja*—[arm wrestling](https://grokipedia.com/page/Arm_wrestling)—into a structured, competitive spectacle.[[1]](#ref-1)[[2]](#ref-2)

Sanctioned by the Indian Arm Wrestling Federation, the league features six city-based franchise teams competing in team formats across men's, women's, and physically challenged categories, with matches contested in various weight classes under standardized rules emphasizing technique, strength, and strategy.[[3]](#ref-3)[[4]](#ref-4)

The inaugural season ran from July 28 to August 13, 2023, across multiple venues including [Delhi](https://grokipedia.com/page/Delhi), culminating in Kochi KDs defeating Kiraak Hyderabad 30–28 in a thrilling final at IGI Stadium, marking the league's debut with widespread broadcast on [Sony Sports Network](https://grokipedia.com/page/Sony_Sports_Network).[[5]](#ref-5)[[6]](#ref-6)

Season 2, held in [Gwalior](https://grokipedia.com/page/Gwalior) in 2025, saw Kiraak Hyderabad claim the championship with a decisive 30–18 victory, further boosting the league's profile and attracting international attention from figures such as [arm wrestling](https://grokipedia.com/page/Arm_wrestling) legend Michael Todd.[[7]](#ref-7)[[4]](#ref-4)

PPL has achieved remarkable popularity, with its first season garnering over 1.2 billion [social media](https://grokipedia.com/page/Social_media) views and the second reaching 250 million households, positioning it as a key driver in professionalizing and globalizing [arm wrestling](https://grokipedia.com/page/Arm_wrestling) in [India](https://grokipedia.com/page/India) while fostering athlete development and advocating for the sport's Olympic inclusion.[[8]](#ref-8)[[9]](#ref-9)[[10]](#ref-10)

## History

### Founding and early development

The Pro Panja League was founded in February 2020 by Swen Entertainment, a media and entertainment company led by Bollywood [actors](https://grokipedia.com/page/Parvin_Dabas) and producers [Parvin Dabas](https://grokipedia.com/page/Parvin_Dabas) and [Preeti Jhangiani](https://grokipedia.com/page/Preeti_Jhangiani).[[1]](#ref-1)[[11]](#ref-11) The initiative aimed to establish India's first franchise-based professional [arm wrestling](https://grokipedia.com/page/Arm_wrestling) league, transforming the traditional sport of *panja* into a structured competitive format with teams, seasons, and broadcast appeal.[[4]](#ref-4)[[11]](#ref-11)

The league's primary goal was to popularize [arm wrestling](https://grokipedia.com/page/Arm_wrestling) across [India](https://grokipedia.com/page/India) by providing a professional platform that highlights athletes' strength, technique, and endurance, while promoting inclusivity for men, women, and differently-abled participants.[[11]](#ref-11)[[1]](#ref-1) It was officially sanctioned by the Indian Arm Wrestling Federation (IAF), ensuring alignment with national standards and eligibility for athletes recognized by the body.[[4]](#ref-4)[[1]](#ref-1) This partnership helped legitimize the league as a key driver for the sport's growth in the country.

Early development faced significant hurdles due to the COVID-19 pandemic, which imposed lockdowns and disrupted in-person events shortly after the launch.[[1]](#ref-1)[[4]](#ref-4) From 2020 to 2022, the founders shifted focus to virtual tournaments and exhibition planning to maintain momentum and scout talent, delaying the inaugural competitive season until 2023.[[11]](#ref-11)

To standardize rules and formats, Swen Entertainment collaborated closely with the IAF and other sports organizations during this period, adapting international [arm wrestling](https://grokipedia.com/page/Arm_wrestling) guidelines to suit a franchise model while emphasizing safety and fairness.[[4]](#ref-4)[[1]](#ref-1) These efforts laid the groundwork for a professional ecosystem, including equal prize structures across categories to encourage broader participation.[[1]](#ref-1)

### Exhibition events and launch preparations

The Pro Panja League initiated a series of [exhibition](https://grokipedia.com/page/Exhibition) events and [ranking](https://grokipedia.com/page/Ranking) tournaments in [2020](https://grokipedia.com/page/2020) to promote [arm wrestling](https://grokipedia.com/page/Arm_wrestling) and identify talent ahead of its formal launch. The first such event, a [ranking](https://grokipedia.com/page/Ranking) tournament, took place on February 29, [2020](https://grokipedia.com/page/2020), at Thyagraj Stadium in [New Delhi](https://grokipedia.com/page/New_Delhi), marking the league's entry into organized competitions and drawing participants from across [India](https://grokipedia.com/page/India).[[12]](#ref-12) These early non-competitive gatherings helped build public interest and served as platforms for testing competition structures during the nascent stages of the league's development.

Amid the [COVID-19 pandemic](https://grokipedia.com/page/COVID-19_pandemic), the league shifted focus to digital engagement, producing promotional videos and online challenges that amassed over 275 million views across [social media](https://grokipedia.com/page/Social_media) platforms in the preceding 10 months leading up to mid-2023. This virtual outreach sustained momentum when in-person events were limited, fostering a broader [audience](https://grokipedia.com/page/Audience) for [arm wrestling](https://grokipedia.com/page/Arm_wrestling) in [India](https://grokipedia.com/page/India) and highlighting athlete stories to inspire participation.[[13]](#ref-13)

A pivotal exhibition event occurred in July 2022 at the historic [Gwalior Fort](https://grokipedia.com/page/Gwalior_Fort) in [Madhya Pradesh](https://grokipedia.com/page/Madhya_Pradesh), where the Pro Panja League hosted its ranking tournament featuring over 300 registered arm wrestlers across multiple weight categories. International arm-wrestling legend Michael Todd, a 23-time world champion from the [United States](https://grokipedia.com/page/United_States), attended as a guest, conducting seminars and witnessing finals that showcased emerging Indian talent, such as Kerala's Tawheed Shaikh and Haryana's Yogesh Chaudhary. The event, held from July 22 to 24, concluded with a special ceremony at the fort premises, emphasizing the league's growing international appeal.[[14]](#ref-14)[[15]](#ref-15)

Preparations for the league's debut intensified with targeted athlete scouting through nationwide registrations and these tournaments, drawing competitors from various regions to form a talent pool for future teams. Rule testing occurred iteratively during these events, with adjustments to weight categories, match formats, and eligibility criteria—such as a 500-gram weigh-in leeway—ensuring fair play and operational smoothness. Franchise ownership rights were secured through private acquisitions, beginning with the sale of teams like [Mumbai](https://grokipedia.com/page/Mumbai) Muscle to [Punit Balan](https://grokipedia.com/page/Punit_Balan) Studios Private Limited and Kiraak Hyderabad to an NG Family-led group in May 2023, establishing the business framework for the competition.[[16]](#ref-16)[[17]](#ref-17)

Key milestones included the [public announcement](https://grokipedia.com/page/Public_Announcement) of the league's IPL-style franchise format in early 2022, which outlined team-based competitions following ranking qualifiers, and the selection of [Indira Gandhi](https://grokipedia.com/page/Indira_Gandhi) Indoor Stadium in [New Delhi](https://grokipedia.com/page/New_Delhi) as the venue for the inaugural season in June 2023. These steps solidified the league's structure, paving the way for its official start in July 2023.[[18]](#ref-18)[[19]](#ref-19)

### Inaugural season and expansion

The inaugural season of the Pro Panja League commenced on July 28, 2023, and concluded on August 13, 2023, at the [Indira Gandhi](https://grokipedia.com/page/Indira_Gandhi) Indoor Stadium in [New Delhi](https://grokipedia.com/page/New_Delhi), marking the official launch of professional [arm wrestling](https://grokipedia.com/page/Arm_wrestling) in [India](https://grokipedia.com/page/India).[[11]](#ref-11) Six franchises—Kiraak Hyderabad, Mumbai Muscle, [Rohtak](https://grokipedia.com/page/Rohtak) Rowdies, [Ludhiana](https://grokipedia.com/page/Ludhiana) Lions, Baroda Badshahs, and [Kochi](https://grokipedia.com/page/Kochi) KD's—competed, with a total of 180 athletes drafted across 10 weight divisions that included men's, women's, and physically challenged categories.[[20]](#ref-20)[[21]](#ref-21) This structure drew participants from diverse regions, fostering broader representation in the sport and attracting 32 million television viewers across [Sony](https://grokipedia.com/page/Sony) Ten 3 and [DD Sports](https://grokipedia.com/page/DD_Sports) channels.[[22]](#ref-22)

Building on this foundation, the league expanded ahead of its second season in 2025, refreshing its franchise lineup to sustain momentum and appeal. New teams such as MP Hathodas and Jaipur Veers were introduced, alongside the rebranding of Ludhiana Lions to Sher-E-Ludhiana, while underperforming or outgoing franchises like Baroda Badshahs and [Kochi](https://grokipedia.com/page/Kochi) KD's were replaced to inject fresh competition.[[3]](#ref-3)[[23]](#ref-23) Retained franchises including Rohtak Rowdies, Kiraak Hyderabad, and Mumbai Muscle continued, maintaining continuity while the overall roster emphasized athletes from varied Indian states, enhancing regional diversity. The inclusion of women's and physically challenged divisions persisted, promoting inclusivity and contributing to heightened [athlete](https://grokipedia.com/page/athlete) participation from underrepresented areas.

Post-2023 developments focused on scaling the league, with Season 2 planning announced in late 2024 and early 2025, culminating in a venue shift to [Gwalior](https://grokipedia.com/page/Gwalior), [Madhya Pradesh](https://grokipedia.com/page/Madhya_Pradesh), for the event hosted at Jiwaji Club.[[24]](#ref-24) This move supported logistical growth and local engagement, as the season achieved expanded viewership reaching 250 million households, underscoring the league's rising impact.[[25]](#ref-25)

In April 2025, the league marked its international debut with the Pro Panja UAE Cup on April 19 at [India](https://grokipedia.com/page/India) Club in [Dubai](https://grokipedia.com/page/Dubai), featuring five high-profile matches, including an [India](https://grokipedia.com/page/India) vs. [Pakistan](https://grokipedia.com/page/Pakistan) showdown between athletes Mazahir Saidu and Umer Awan, sanctioned by the Dubai Sports Council and highlighting the league's growing global presence.[[26]](#ref-26)[[27]](#ref-27)

## Rules and format

### League structure

The Pro Panja League operates with six franchise teams competing in a structured [tournament](https://grokipedia.com/page/Tournament) format designed to build competitive intensity over a condensed period. The league consists of a round-robin phase followed by [playoffs](https://grokipedia.com/page/Playoffs), with all matches contested in various weight categories across men's, women's, and para divisions.[[4]](#ref-4)[[28]](#ref-28)

In the round-robin phase, each of the six [team](https://grokipedia.com/page/Team)s faces every other [team](https://grokipedia.com/page/Team) twice, once in each "home" and "away" configuration, though all [events](https://grokipedia.com/page/2000_in_anime) are hosted at a central venue to streamline logistics and spectator access. Teams accumulate points based on the outcomes of these matches, with victories in individual bouts contributing to overall team standings; the system includes base points for rounds won along with bonus and championship points for strategic performances. This phase determines the league table, emphasizing consistent performance across multiple encounters.[[28]](#ref-28)[[29]](#ref-29)

The top four teams from the round-robin standings advance to the [playoffs](https://grokipedia.com/page/Playoffs), which feature a straightforward elimination [bracket](https://grokipedia.com/page/Bracket) without a third-place match. The semi-finals pit the first-placed team against the fourth and the second against the third, with winners proceeding to a single [grand final](https://grokipedia.com/page/Grand_final) to decide the champion. The entire season typically spans 17 to 20 days of competitive action, allowing for rapid progression from group stage to conclusion in a single host city, such as [Gwalior](https://grokipedia.com/page/Gwalior) for the 2025 season.[[30]](#ref-30)[[31]](#ref-31)[[32]](#ref-32)

### Match gameplay

Matches in the Pro Panja League are [arm wrestling](https://grokipedia.com/page/Arm_wrestling) bouts between two players from opposing teams, matched within the same weight category to ensure fair competition. Each bout is structured as either an undercard (preliminary) match, consisting of three rounds, or a main card match, consisting of five rounds, with each round concluding upon a pin.[[33]](#ref-33)[[34]](#ref-34)

The scoring system awards 1 point to the winning team for each round their player secures by pinning the opponent's hand to the touch pad. A clean sweep—winning all three rounds in an undercard bout or all five in a main card bout—grants an additional 2 bonus points to the victorious team. Total team points for a bout are calculated as the sum of game points from rounds won plus any applicable bonuses.[[33]](#ref-33)[[35]](#ref-35)

If a bout ends in a tie after the standard rounds, an optional Bullet Badshah Round serves as a sudden-death [tiebreaker](https://grokipedia.com/page/Tiebreaker), where the first player to achieve a pin wins the bout and earns 5 bonus points for their team. This round is typically called by a star player from the trailing or tied team to potentially shift the outcome.[[33]](#ref-33)[[35]](#ref-35)[[36]](#ref-36)

Refereeing follows standard arm wrestling protocols, emphasizing fair play through enforced rules on grip and positioning. Competitors begin with a standard handshake grip, supervised by the referee to ensure neutrality, while the non-dominant elbows must remain aligned and fixed on the elbow pad throughout the bout to avoid fouls. A valid pin requires the opponent's hand to touch the pad fully, with referees monitoring for infractions such as slipping or improper leverage.[[37]](#ref-37)[[38]](#ref-38)

### Divisions and categories

The Pro Panja League organizes competitions into three primary divisions—men's, women's, and physically challenged—to promote equitable matchups based on [gender](https://grokipedia.com/page/Gender), physical capability, and body weight. These divisions ensure that athletes compete against peers with similar attributes, fostering fair play and reducing injury risks in [arm wrestling](https://grokipedia.com/page/Arm_wrestling) bouts. The structure draws from international [arm wrestling](https://grokipedia.com/page/Arm_wrestling) standards while adapting to promote inclusivity in the Indian context.[[39]](#ref-39)

Within the men's division, athletes are categorized into six weight classes: under 60 kg, 60-70 kg, 70-80 kg, 80-90 kg, 90-100 kg, and over 100 kg. The women's division features three adjusted classes: under 55 kg, 55-65 kg, and over 65 kg, accounting for typical physiological differences. The physically challenged division operates as an open category for para-athletes with disabilities, allowing participation without strict weight limits to emphasize accessibility over rigid classification. These categories, totaling 10 across divisions, enable teams to draft balanced rosters of 30 players each.[[40]](#ref-40)

Eligibility for participation requires athletes to be scouted through national trials and ranking tournaments across [India](https://grokipedia.com/page/India), with priority given to Indian citizens or [residents](https://grokipedia.com/page/The_Residents) to build a homegrown talent ecosystem. The league, sanctioned by the Indian Arm Wrestling Federation, conducts open registrations and [scouting](https://grokipedia.com/page/Scouting) events in major cities to identify promising players, ensuring a diverse pool from various regions.[[41]](#ref-41)

The women's division was introduced alongside the men's in the inaugural 2023 season to encourage [female](https://grokipedia.com/page/Female) participation in a traditionally male-dominated [sport](https://grokipedia.com/page/Sport), while the physically challenged category was established from the league's launch to advance inclusivity for athletes with disabilities. By the 2025 season, these divisions had expanded representation, with para-athletes gaining dedicated visibility in broadcasts and events.[[42]](#ref-42)[[39]](#ref-39)

## Teams

### Current teams

The Pro Panja League operates on a franchise model, where team owners acquire franchises through bidding processes and invest in player auctions, contracts, and team branding to build competitive rosters.[[16]](#ref-16)[[43]](#ref-43) For the 2025 season, six teams competed, representing various Indian cities and featuring a mix of established and emerging talent.[[3]](#ref-3)[[23]](#ref-23)

| Rank | Team | Matches Played | Games Points | Bonus + Challenger Points | Total Points |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | Kiraak Hyderabad | 11 | 123 | 100 | 223 |
| 2 | [Jaipur Veers](https://grokipedia.com/page/Jaipur) | 12 | 98 | 76 | 174 |
| 3 | [Rohtak Rowdies](https://grokipedia.com/page/Rohtak) | 12 | 104 | 68 | 172 |
| 4 | MP Hathodas | 11 | 84 | 61 | 145 |
| 5 | Sher-E-Ludhiana | 10 | 70 | 51 | 121 |
| 6 | [Mumbai Muscle](https://grokipedia.com/page/Mumbai) | 10 | 56 | 49 | 105 |

**Kiraak Hyderabad**, based in Hyderabad, [Telangana](https://grokipedia.com/page/Telangana), is owned by Ned Ventures led by Gautam Reddy and topped the 2025 points table with 223 total points (TP), culminating in a championship victory over [Rohtak](https://grokipedia.com/page/Rohtak) Rowdies by a score of 30-18.[[16]](#ref-16)[[4]](#ref-4)[[44]](#ref-44) The team, led by CEO Trinadh Reddy, features star player [Satnam Singh](https://grokipedia.com/page/Satnam_Singh), who earned Player of the Tournament honors. Stewe Thomas broke league records, including a 0.09-second pin, and won the 'Badshaho ka Badshah' award.[[23]](#ref-23)[[44]](#ref-44)[[45]](#ref-45) Other notable roster members include Savita Kumari, a top-ranked competitor in the women's division.[[40]](#ref-40)

**Jaipur Veers**, hailing from [Jaipur](https://grokipedia.com/page/Jaipur), [Rajasthan](https://grokipedia.com/page/Rajasthan), entered the league in 2025 and finished second in the standings with 174 TP and strong performances across multiple weight categories.[[23]](#ref-23)[[4]](#ref-4) The franchise emphasizes regional talent development, though specific ownership details for the 2025 season remain undisclosed in public records.

**Rohtak Rowdies**, representing [Rohtak](https://grokipedia.com/page/Rohtak), Haryana, made their league debut in 2025 under owner [Abhishek Malik](https://grokipedia.com/page/Abhishek_Malik), finished third in the league stage with 172 TP, and reached the finals for a runner-up finish.[[23]](#ref-23)[[4]](#ref-4) Key players include Asian and World Champion Srinivas BV, who dominated the specially-abled category and earned Player of the Day accolades.[[46]](#ref-46)

**MP Hathodas**, from [Madhya Pradesh](https://grokipedia.com/page/Madhya_Pradesh), secured fourth place with 145 TP in 2025, focusing on robust defenses in heavier weight classes.[[4]](#ref-4) Ownership information is not publicly detailed, but the team highlights endurance-based strategies in its branding.

**Sher-E-Ludhiana**, based in [Ludhiana](https://grokipedia.com/page/Ludhiana), [Punjab](https://grokipedia.com/page/Punjab), placed fifth with 121 TP and contributions from international-caliber athletes like Senebi, a top-ranked player in the [ranking](https://grokipedia.com/page/Ranking)s.[[4]](#ref-4)[[40]](#ref-40) The franchise invests heavily in cross-border talent to bolster its competitive edge.

**Mumbai Muscle**, from [Mumbai](https://grokipedia.com/page/Mumbai), [Maharashtra](https://grokipedia.com/page/Maharashtra), is owned by Punit Balan Studios Private Limited and rounded out the standings in sixth with 105 TP, led by high-performer Teja P in the rankings.[[16]](#ref-16)[[4]](#ref-4)[[40]](#ref-40) The team draws on urban backing to promote [arm wrestling](https://grokipedia.com/page/Arm_wrestling) in metropolitan areas.

### Former teams

The Pro Panja League's former teams consist of franchises that participated in the inaugural 2023 season or were announced for subsequent seasons but ceased operations or were not retained.

[Kochi](https://grokipedia.com/page/Kochi) KD's, [Ludhiana](https://grokipedia.com/page/Ludhiana) Lions, and Baroda Badshahs competed in the 2023 season.[[47]](#ref-47)

[Kochi](https://grokipedia.com/page/Kochi) KD's claimed the inaugural title with a 30–28 victory over Kiraak Hyderabad in the final.[[48]](#ref-48)

Ludhiana Lions underwent a [rebranding](https://grokipedia.com/page/Rebranding) to Sher-e-[Ludhiana](https://grokipedia.com/page/Ludhiana) ahead of the 2025 season.[[49]](#ref-49)

Baroda Badshahs did not return for the 2025 season.[[23]](#ref-23)

Rarh [Bengal](https://grokipedia.com/page/Bengal) Tigers was introduced as a new eastern [India](https://grokipedia.com/page/India) franchise in July 2024 but dissolved before competing in season 2.[[50]](#ref-50)

Several players from these teams transitioned to active franchises, including members of Kochi KD's joining [Jaipur](https://grokipedia.com/page/Jaipur) Veers.[[51]](#ref-51)

## Seasons and results

### 2023 season

The 2023 Pro Panja League season marked the inaugural edition of the professional [arm wrestling](https://grokipedia.com/page/Arm_wrestling) competition in [India](https://grokipedia.com/page/India), featuring six teams—Kiraak Hyderabad, Kochi KD's, Mumbai Muscle, [Rohtak](https://grokipedia.com/page/Rohtak) Rowdies, [Ludhiana](https://grokipedia.com/page/Ludhiana) Lions, and Baroda Badshahs—competing in a round-robin format followed by [playoffs](https://grokipedia.com/page/Playoffs). The event ran from July 28 to August 13 at the Indira Gandhi Indoor Stadium in [New Delhi](https://grokipedia.com/page/New_Delhi).[[52]](#ref-52)[[53]](#ref-53)[[54]](#ref-54)

During the league stage, teams accumulated total points (TP) based on wins in various weight categories and bout formats. Kiraak Hyderabad topped the standings with 78 TP, securing strong performances across multiple divisions, while Kochi KD's followed with 64 TP, highlighted by consistent victories in key matches. [Rohtak](https://grokipedia.com/page/Rohtak) Rowdies earned 61 TP, [Ludhiana](https://grokipedia.com/page/Ludhiana) Lions 57 TP, [Mumbai](https://grokipedia.com/page/Mumbai) Muscle 49 TP, and Baroda Badshahs trailed with lower totals, setting the stage for intense playoff qualification. Notable debut wins included dominant category sweeps by players from Kiraak Hyderabad, contributing to their lead.[[55]](#ref-55)[[56]](#ref-56)

The top four teams advanced to the playoffs. On August 12, the semi-finals saw Kiraak Hyderabad defeat Rohtak Rowdies 20-18, with standout wins in the main card bouts securing their spot in the final. In the other semi-final, [Kochi KD's](https://grokipedia.com/page/Kochi) overcame Mumbai Muscle, advancing through a series of decisive category victories. The final on August 13 pitted [Kochi KD's](https://grokipedia.com/page/Kochi) against Kiraak Hyderabad, culminating in a close 30-28 victory for [Kochi KD's](https://grokipedia.com/page/Kochi) after a tied main card score of 27-27, decided by undercard points. This marked [Kochi KD's](https://grokipedia.com/page/Kochi) as the first champions, clinching the title in a thrilling showdown.[[57]](#ref-57)[[6]](#ref-6)[[48]](#ref-48)

The season introduced innovations such as the first inclusion of women's bouts alongside men's and specially-abled categories, promoting inclusivity in the sport. The total points system was applied across all matches to determine outcomes, emphasizing cumulative performance over individual bouts. While specific [stadium](https://grokipedia.com/page/Stadium) [attendance](https://grokipedia.com/page/Attendance) figures were not publicly detailed, [the event](https://grokipedia.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

**Current season, competition or edition:** ![Current sports event](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/Sports_current_event.svg/40px-Sports_current_event.svg.png) *[2025 Pro Panja League](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=2025_Pro_Panja_League&action=edit&redlink=1)*

| Sport | [Arm wrestling](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arm_wrestling) |
| --- | --- |
| Founded | 2020 |
| First season | 2023 |
| Motto | Aapki Kismat, Aapke Haath (Your Fate, Your Hands) Bharat Ka Khel (India's Game) |
| No. of teams | 6 |
| Country | [India](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/India) |
| Most recent champion | Kiraak Hyderabad |
| Most titles | Rohtak Rowdies Kochi KD's (1 Title each) |
| Broadcasters | (Main Card) [Sony Sports Network](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sony_Sports_Network) [DD Sports](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/DD_Sports) |
| Streaming partners | India: [Fancode](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fancode) Worldwide: SportVot |
| Official website | [https://propanja.com/](https://propanja.com/) |

Seasons

- [2023](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2023_Pro_Panja_League)

The **Pro Panja League** (**PPL**)[[1]](#cite_note-1) is a professional Indian [arm wrestling](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arm_wrestling) league which had its first season in July 2023. There are separate competition categories for men, women, and specially abled (disabled) players,[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2) and the tournament took place at [Indira Gandhi Indoor Stadium](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indira_Gandhi_Indoor_Stadium) in New Delhi.[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3)

## History

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Pro_Panja_League&action=edit&section=1)]

The name of the tournament is based on "Panja", a word used in the [Indian subcontinent](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_subcontinent) to refer to arm wrestling.[[4]](#cite_note-4) Starting in 2020, there were some exhibition events, tournaments, and matches held,[[5]](#cite_note-5) which acquired about 275 million views on social media.[[6]](#cite_note-:2-6) Before the first season, there was a ranking tournament held at [Gwalior, Madhya Pradesh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gwalior,_Madhya_Pradesh),[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2) with the top 18 arm wrestlers in each of the 10 weight categories qualifying for the first season.[[6]](#cite_note-:2-6) The first season was won by the Kochi KDs.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## Rules

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Pro_Panja_League&action=edit&section=2)]

### League format

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Pro_Panja_League&action=edit&section=3)]

Each team plays each other twice during the round-robin stage, and the top four teams enter the playoffs, which consist of two semi-finals (1st vs 4th, 2nd vs 3rd) and a final.

### Gameplay

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Pro_Panja_League&action=edit&section=4)]

Two players in the same weight category compete to overpower each other and score points over several rounds of a bout. In an "under card/prelim" bout, three rounds are contested, while in a "main card" bout, five rounds are contested. Three under card and three main card bouts take place within a single match, each featuring a different pair of opponents; the team whose players scored more points wins the match. In an under card bout, the player who wins two out of three rounds earns one point, while the loser gains nothing; in a main card bout, players earns one point for every round they win.[[8]](#cite_note-:3-8) In the final, five main card bouts are contested.[[9]](#cite_note-:4-9)

At the start of each round, the players offer one of their hands while keeping elbows on a specially designed table, with a strap being bound around offered hands to avoid slippage. The players attempt to force their opponent's hand to hit designated touch pads on the table underneath their hands to win the round. There are various rule violations which can result in penalties against players.[[10]](#cite_note-10) Two bonus points are gained in main card bout if a player wins without opponent scoring a point (i.e. a 3-0 victory earns 5 points).[[8]](#cite_note-:3-8) Bout must be completed within 30 minutes. An additional challenge round can be called in main card bout. The player calling for the challenge round can win five bonus points if they beat opponent within 10 seconds; however, if it fails, the opponent earns five bonus points.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[8]](#cite_note-:3-8)[[9]](#cite_note-:4-9)

## Teams

Six teams compete in the league. 180 players in ten different weight categories were drafted for the first season,[[12]](#cite_note-12) with six weight categories reserved for men, three for women, and one for physically challenged participants.[[13]](#cite_note-13) The winning team will win ₹2 million (US$24,000), while the runner-up team will win ₹500,000 (US$5,900), with equal money distributed in all categories.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

## Broadcasters

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Pro_Panja_League&action=edit&section=9)]

Pro Panja League telecasts on [Sony Sports Network](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sony_Sports_Network) and [DD Sports](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/DD_Sports) in India.[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3) It also broadcasts on [FanCode](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/FanCode) and SportVot.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

Betzoid Argentina Revela Datos Históricos sobre Depósitos Mínimos en Casinos La industria del juego en Argentina ha experimentado transformaciones significativas durante las últimas décadas, especialmente en lo que respecta a la accesibilidad y democratización de los casinos en línea. Betzoid Argentina, reconocida plataforma de análisis y comparación de operadores de juego, ha compilado información histórica reveladora sobre la evolución de los depósitos mínimos en casinos digitales. Este análisis proporciona una perspectiva única sobre cómo las barreras de entrada han cambiado con el tiempo, reflejando tanto avances tecnológicos como cambios en las estrategias comerciales de los operadores. Comprender esta evolución resulta fundamental para contextualizar el estado actual del mercado argentino y las tendencias que continúan moldeando la experiencia de los jugadores en el país. ## Los Primeros Años: Barreras de Entrada Elevadas (2005-2012) Durante los primeros años de los casinos en línea que operaban para el mercado argentino, los depósitos mínimos representaban una barrera considerable para muchos jugadores potenciales. Entre 2005 y 2012, la mayoría de los operadores internacionales que aceptaban jugadores argentinos establecían requisitos de depósito inicial que oscilaban entre el equivalente a 50 y 100 dólares estadounidenses. En términos de pesos argentinos de aquella época, estas cantidades representaban una inversión significativa para el jugador promedio.Betzoid Argentina destaca que esta situación se debía principalmente a limitaciones tecnológicas en los sistemas de pago y a los costos operativos asociados con el procesamiento de transacciones internacionales. Los métodos de pago disponibles eran limitados, predominando las transferencias bancarias internacionales y algunas tarjetas de crédito específicas. Estos canales implicaban comisiones elevadas que los operadores trasladaban indirectamente a los usuarios mediante requisitos de depósito más altos.Adicionalmente, la falta de regulación específica en Argentina para el juego en línea durante este período generaba incertidumbre tanto para operadores como para jugadores. Los casinos internacionales compensaban el riesgo regulatorio estableciendo umbrales de entrada más elevados, buscando atraer únicamente a jugadores con mayor capacidad de gasto y, presumiblemente, mayor compromiso con la plataforma. ## La Transición hacia la Accesibilidad (2013-2018) El período comprendido entre 2013 y 2018 marcó un punto de inflexión en la industria. Según los datos recopilados por Betzoid Argentina, los depósitos mínimos comenzaron a descender gradualmente, reflejando tanto la mayor competencia en el mercado como la incorporación de nuevas tecnologías de pago. La llegada de billeteras electrónicas como Skrill, Neteller y posteriormente soluciones locales como Mercado Pago, revolucionó la forma en que los argentinos podían acceder a los casinos en línea.Durante esta etapa, los depósitos mínimos se redujeron a rangos entre 10 y 20 dólares, lo que en pesos argentinos representaba montos considerablemente más accesibles. Esta democratización del acceso coincidió con el crecimiento exponencial de la penetración de internet en Argentina y la masificación de los dispositivos móviles, factores que expandieron significativamente la base potencial de jugadores.Betzoid Argentina también documenta que este período vio el surgimiento de los primeros operadores especializados en mercados latinoamericanos, quienes comprendían mejor las particularidades económicas de la región. Estos operadores implementaron estrategias más agresivas de adquisición de clientes, utilizando depósitos mínimos reducidos como herramienta competitiva para ganar participación de mercado frente a los gigantes internacionales tradicionales.La volatilidad cambiaria que caracterizó a Argentina durante estos años también influyó en las políticas de depósito. Los operadores debieron adaptar constantemente sus umbrales mínimos para mantener la accesibilidad en términos reales, lo que resultó en ajustes frecuentes que reflejaban las fluctuaciones del peso argentino frente a las monedas de referencia internacional. ## La Era Moderna: Microdepósitos y Segmentación (2019-Actualidad) Desde 2019 hasta la actualidad, el mercado argentino de casinos en línea ha presenciado una sofisticación sin precedentes en las estrategias de depósito mínimo. Los hallazgos de Betzoid Argentina revelan que la tendencia predominante ha sido la segmentación de ofertas, donde diferentes operadores se posicionan para distintos perfiles de jugadores mediante estructuras de depósito variadas.Actualmente, el espectro de depósitos mínimos en el mercado argentino es extraordinariamente amplio. Mientras algunos operadores premium mantienen requisitos de entrada más elevados asociados con programas VIP y bonificaciones exclusivas, una cantidad creciente de plataformas ha adoptado políticas de microdepósitos. En este contexto, los [casinos depósito mínimo 2000 pesos](https://betzoid.com/ar/deposito-minimo-2000-ars/) se han convertido en una opción particularmente popular entre operadores que buscan equilibrar accesibilidad con sostenibilidad operativa, ofreciendo un punto de entrada que resulta alcanzable para una amplia base de jugadores sin comprometer la viabilidad económica del servicio.La implementación de criptomonedas como método de pago ha introducido una nueva dimensión en la discusión sobre depósitos mínimos. Bitcoin, Ethereum y otras criptomonedas permiten transacciones con montos extremadamente bajos debido a la eliminación de intermediarios tradicionales. Betzoid Argentina documenta que algunos operadores especializados en criptomonedas ofrecen depósitos mínimos equivalentes a apenas 5 dólares, aunque la volatilidad inherente a estos activos digitales presenta desafíos únicos para los jugadores argentinos.La pandemia de COVID-19 aceleró tendencias que ya estaban en desarrollo. Con más argentinos confinados en sus hogares y buscando opciones de entretenimiento digital, los operadores respondieron con políticas de depósito aún más accesibles. Betzoid Argentina observa que durante 2020 y 2021, varios operadores lanzaron promociones especiales con depósitos mínimos temporalmente reducidos, algunas alcanzando niveles históricos de apenas 500 pesos argentinos. ## Factores Económicos y Regulatorios que Moldean el Presente El análisis de Betzoid Argentina no estaría completo sin considerar los factores macroeconómicos y regulatorios que continúan influyendo en las políticas de depósito mínimo. La inflación persistente en Argentina representa un desafío constante para los operadores, quienes deben actualizar regularmente sus umbrales de depósito para mantener su valor real en términos de poder adquisitivo.Las provincias argentinas han comenzado a desarrollar marcos regulatorios propios para el juego en línea, siendo Buenos Aires y Ciudad de Buenos Aires pioneras en este aspecto. Estos marcos regulatorios incluyen disposiciones específicas sobre protección al consumidor que, indirectamente, influyen en las políticas de depósito. Por ejemplo, los requisitos de verificación de identidad y las medidas de juego responsable pueden afectar la estructura de depósitos que los operadores implementan.Betzoid Argentina también señala que las restricciones cambiarias implementadas en diferentes momentos de la historia reciente argentina han tenido impactos significativos. Las limitaciones para adquirir divisas extranjeras han llevado a algunos jugadores a buscar operadores que acepten exclusivamente pesos argentinos, lo que ha estimulado el desarrollo de soluciones de pago localizadas con estructuras de depósito adaptadas a la realidad económica nacional.

Indian Arm Wrestling Federation

- [Article](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_Arm_Wrestling_Federation)
- [Talk](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Talk:Indian_Arm_Wrestling_Federation)

**Indian Arm Wrestling Federation** is the [governing body](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Governing_body) for [arm wrestling](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arm_wrestling) in [India](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/India).

| Sport | [Arm Wrestling](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arm_Wrestling) |
| --- | --- |
| Jurisdiction | [India](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/India) |
| Founded | 1977 |
| Headquarters | [Chhattisgarh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chhattisgarh) |

Official website

[indianarmwrestling.com](http://indianarmwrestling.com/)

## Contents

- [1 History](#History)
- [2 Tournaments](#Tournaments)
- [3 Competitions](#Competitions)
- [4 See also](#See_also)
- [5 References](#References)
- [6 External links](#External_links)

## History

[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Indian_Arm_Wrestling_Federation&action=edit&section=1)

Indian Arm Wrestling Federation was formed in 1977 to promote Arm Wrestling as sport in India.[[1]](#cite_note-ia1-1) Arm Wrestling also called as "Panja" has been promoted through Pro Panja League (PPL) set up in Year 2020[[2]](#cite_note-ia6-2) for organising League matches.[[3]](#cite_note-ia3-3)[[4]](#cite_note-ia2-4)

## Tournaments

[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Indian_Arm_Wrestling_Federation&action=edit&section=2)

Indian Arm Wrestling Federation conducted 45th Arm Wrestling Championship in Srinagar with the support of Jammu and Kashmir Arm Wrestling Association.[[5]](#cite_note-ia5-5)[[6]](#cite_note-ia7-6)

Indian Arm Wrestling Federation has also been organising regular league level matches across India every year.[[7]](#cite_note-ia8-7)[[8]](#cite_note-ia9-8)

## Competitions

[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Indian_Arm_Wrestling_Federation&action=edit&section=3)

Indian Arm Wrestling Federation sent 43 member team to World Arm Wrestling Championships held at Almaty, Kazaksthan from August 24 to September 3.[[9]](#cite_note-ia4-9)
